title: Ouden OS Plan
shortDescription: Windows optimization plan for Ouden OS
description: A review-first Windows optimization playbook that stages cleanup, privacy hardening, and performance tuning through a guided wizard.
details: Ouden OS scans the current install, resolves a profile-aware plan, and packages the same decisions for wizard and ISO flows.

chapters.title: Optimization Level
chapters.description: Choose how aggressive the optimization should be.

questions.label: Optimization Level
questions.title: How aggressively should we optimize your system?
questions.desc: This preset controls which advanced questions appear later. Conservative is safe for anyone. Expert unlocks every tweak — including ones with real trade-offs.
questions.note: You can always return and change this. Questions hidden at lower presets are just deferred, not locked.

options.title: Conservative — safe cleanup only
options.desc: Light cleanup and privacy fixes. Nothing that changes system behavior or touches security settings. Best for shared or work PCs.

options.title: Balanced — good for most systems
options.desc: A solid mix of performance, privacy, and stability improvements. Avoids anything that needs manual reversal.

options.title: Aggressive — gaming / enthusiast
options.desc: Unlocks deeper tweaks: memory compression, core parking, GPU P-state locks. Aimed at dedicated gaming rigs.

options.title: Expert — full control
options.desc: Every question is shown. Includes Spectre/Meltdown mitigation toggles, full Windows Update control, and Defender disablement. You accept the risks.

warnings: Expert preset enables questions that can significantly reduce system security. Only proceed if you understand the implications.

chapters.title: Essential Tweaks
chapters.description: Core system changes — highest impact, lowest risk.

questions.label: Power Plan
questions.title: Switch to the High Performance power plan?
questions.desc: The default Balanced plan throttles CPU speed to save power. High Performance keeps the CPU at full clock, eliminating latency spikes. performance research calls this the single most impactful change for gaming responsiveness.
questions.note: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power — sets the active plan GUID to 8c5e7fda-e8bf-4a96-9a85-a6e23a8c635c.

options.title: Yes — switch to High Performance
options.desc: Full CPU clock at all times. Eliminates frequency ramp-up latency that causes frame-time spikes in games.

options.title: No — keep Balanced
options.desc: Windows default. Good for laptops on battery. CPU may throttle during bursty workloads.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the Balanced power plan.

questions.label: Fast Startup
questions.title: Disable Fast Startup (Hybrid Boot)?
questions.desc: Fast Startup saves a hibernation snapshot of the kernel on shutdown. This can cause stale drivers, hidden power states, and misleading 'uptime' readings. Both performance research and community benchmarks recommend disabling it for clean boots.
questions.note: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Power — HiberbootEnabled = 0.

options.title: Yes — disable Fast Startup
options.desc: Full cold boot on every shutdown. Slightly longer POST, but drivers always load fresh and hibernation snapshots can't interfere.

options.title: No — keep Fast Startup
options.desc: Faster Windows shutdown/resume cycle. May cause driver state issues after updates.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Fast Startup enabled.

questions.label: Hibernation
questions.title: Disable hibernation and remove hiberfil.sys?
questions.desc: Hibernation writes a full RAM snapshot to disk (hiberfil.sys). On a 16 GB+ system this file can be 8–16 GB. If you never hibernate, this is wasted space and pointless I/O on shutdown. performance research runs `powercfg /h off` as a standard step.
questions.note: Runs: powercfg /hibernate off — removes C:\hiberfil.sys and disables the hibernate power state.

options.title: Yes — disable hibernation
options.desc: Frees hiberfil.sys disk space and removes the hibernate power option. Fast Startup also stops working (which is already disabled above).

options.title: No — keep hibernation
options.desc: Useful on laptops where you close the lid for long periods. Preserves hiberfil.sys.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep hibernation enabled.

questions.label: Search Indexing
questions.title: Disable Windows Search indexing (WSearch service)?
questions.desc: The Windows Search service continuously indexes files in the background, consuming CPU and disk I/O. On an SSD where file access is already fast, the index provides minimal benefit while generating steady background load. Both performance research and community benchmarks recommend disabling it.
questions.note: Sets WSearch service to disabled and stops it. HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\WSearch — Start = 4.

options.title: Yes — disable indexing
options.desc: Stops background I/O from the indexer. Search still works, just without pre-built index (slightly slower first results in Explorer).

options.title: No — keep indexing
options.desc: Instant results in Windows Search. Useful on HDDs or if you search files heavily.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows Search indexing active.

questions.label: Background App Access
questions.title: Block apps from running in the background?
questions.desc: Windows lets Store apps receive data, send notifications, and stay active when not focused. Disabling this via AppPrivacy policy reduces unnecessary CPU wake-ups and network calls. Note: on Windows 11 24H2, this setting moved into Explorer and may require a reboot to take effect.
questions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppPrivacy — LetAppsRunInBackground = 2.

options.title: Yes — block background app activity
options.desc: Apps only run when focused. Reduces CPU wake-ups and network calls from idle apps. Effective after reboot on 24H2+.

warnings: On Windows 11 24H2+, this policy moved to Explorer. A reboot is required for the change to take full effect.

options.title: No — allow background app activity
options.desc: Apps can receive updates and notifications in the background. Default Windows behaviour.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to allow apps to run in the background.

questions.label: SysMain / Superfetch
questions.title: Disable SysMain (Superfetch) service?
questions.desc: SysMain pre-loads frequently used apps into RAM to speed up launches. On an SSD, load times are already near-instant, so the service just burns RAM and generates background disk I/O with no real benefit. performance research marks this as a standard SSD optimisation.
questions.note: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\SysMain — Start = 4. The service is also stopped immediately.

options.title: Yes — disable SysMain
options.desc: Frees the RAM SysMain reserves for its cache. No practical downside on SSDs.

options.title: No — keep SysMain
options.desc: Useful on HDDs where pre-loading apps reduces perceptible launch lag.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep SysMain enabled.

questions.label: Automatic Maintenance
questions.title: Disable Windows Automatic Maintenance?
questions.desc: Windows schedules maintenance tasks (disk defrag, Windows Defender scans, diagnostics) to run at 2 AM by default, but it can also trigger mid-session if the system was idle. This causes sudden CPU and disk spikes that hurt frame times. Both community benchmarks and performance research recommend disabling it.
questions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\Maintenance — MaintenanceDisabled = 1.

options.title: Yes — disable automatic maintenance
options.desc: Prevents unexpected background scans and defrag runs. You can still trigger maintenance manually from Settings.

options.title: No — keep automatic maintenance
options.desc: Windows runs health tasks on a schedule. Can cause performance spikes if it triggers while you're gaming.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep automatic maintenance enabled.

questions.label: Thread Scheduling
questions.title: Optimize Win32PrioritySeparation for foreground responsiveness?
questions.desc: Win32PrioritySeparation controls how much the scheduler boosts foreground thread priority over background threads. The default value (2) is balanced. performance research recommends 0x26 (38) which gives foreground threads a longer, higher-priority quantum — directly reducing input lag in games.
questions.note: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\PriorityControl — Win32PrioritySeparation = 38 (0x26). Also sets csrss.exe base priority to high.

options.title: Yes — boost foreground thread priority
options.desc: Foreground app (your game) gets longer CPU time slices and higher scheduler priority. Background tasks yield faster.

options.title: No — keep default scheduling
options.desc: Windows default balanced scheduler. Fine for multi-tasking workloads.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the default Win32PrioritySeparation value.

questions.label: Print Spooler
questions.title: Do you use a printer on this PC?
questions.desc: The Print Spooler service runs even when no printer is attached. If you don't print from this machine, disabling it removes an unnecessary service and closes several historical RPC attack surfaces.
questions.note: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Spooler — Start = 4. This also disables the Fax service.

options.title: No printer — disable Print Spooler
options.desc: Stops the Spooler and Fax services. Printing will not work unless you re-enable the service.

options.title: Yes — keep Print Spooler
options.desc: Required for any local or network printing. Keep this if you use a printer.

behavior.blockReason: You indicated this PC uses a printer.

questions.label: Remote Desktop / Phone Service
questions.title: Do you use Remote Desktop or phone-linking on this PC?
questions.desc: Remote Desktop and the Phone-Link (Phone Service) run background listeners. If you don't connect to this PC remotely or sync a phone, these services are pure overhead and attack surface.
questions.note: Disables services.disable-remote-services and services.disable-phone-service.

options.title: No — disable remote services
options.desc: Stops Remote Desktop and Phone Service. You won't be able to RDP into this machine.

options.title: Yes — keep remote access
options.desc: Keeps Remote Desktop and Phone Link services running. Required for remote management or phone sync.

behavior.blockReason: You indicated this PC uses remote access or phone linking.

questions.label: AutoPlay
questions.title: Disable AutoPlay for removable drives?
questions.desc: AutoPlay automatically runs content from USB drives and optical discs when inserted. This is a classic malware delivery vector and provides no real convenience benefit that File Explorer doesn't already cover. Both repos recommend disabling it.
questions.note: HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\AutoplayHandlers — DisableAutoplay = 1.

options.title: Yes — disable AutoPlay
options.desc: Nothing happens automatically when a USB drive is plugged in. You open it manually via File Explorer.

options.title: No — keep AutoPlay
options.desc: Windows prompts you to open, play, or import content when a drive is plugged in.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep AutoPlay enabled.

questions.label: NTFS Last Access Time
questions.title: Disable NTFS last-access timestamp updates?
questions.desc: By default, Windows writes a timestamp to every file it reads. On a busy system this creates a constant stream of small writes even during read-only operations. performance research recommends disabling this via `fsutil` for meaningful I/O reduction on mechanical drives and SSDs alike.
questions.note: Runs: fsutil behavior set disablelastaccess 1 — also enables storage.ntfs-optimization for further NTFS tuning.

options.title: Yes — disable last-access writes
options.desc: Reading a file no longer triggers a metadata write. Reduces NTFS overhead, especially on HDDs.

options.title: No — keep last-access timestamps
options.desc: Every file read updates its last-access time. Useful for backup software that relies on access timestamps.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep NTFS last-access timestamps.

questions.label: SVCHost Splitting
questions.title: Set svchost process split threshold based on RAM?
questions.desc: On systems with 4 GB+ RAM, Windows can run each Windows service in its own svchost.exe process. This isolates them for better stability and makes Task Manager readable. community benchmarks enables this by default on modern RAM amounts.
questions.note: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control — SvcHostSplitThresholdInKB = physical RAM in KB (e.g. 8388608 for 8 GB). Enables perf.svchost-split-threshold.

options.title: Yes — split services into individual processes
options.desc: Each service gets its own svchost.exe. Easier to diagnose CPU/memory hogs and slightly more crash-isolated.

options.title: No — keep grouped svchost
options.desc: Windows default: services share svchost processes. Slightly lower process count.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the default svchost grouping.

questions.label: Fault Tolerant Heap
questions.title: Disable Fault Tolerant Heap (FTH)?
questions.desc: FTH monitors apps that crash and silently applies compatibility shims to prevent them crashing again. The shims include artificial delays that cap performance — performance research has measured FTH secretly throttling game frame rates. If your apps are stable, FTH is pure overhead.
questions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\FTH — Enabled = 0. This does NOT fix unstable apps; if an app needs FTH, disabling it will make the app crash more visibly.

options.title: Yes — disable FTH
options.desc: Removes the compatibility delay shims. Apps will crash normally instead of being silently throttled.

warnings: If a game or app relied on FTH shims, it may crash more often. Only disable if your system is stable.

options.title: No — keep FTH enabled
options.desc: Windows continues to protect unstable apps with compatibility shims. Safe default.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Fault Tolerant Heap enabled.

chapters.title: Privacy & Telemetry
chapters.description: Remove Microsoft telemetry and data collection.

questions.label: Telemetry Level
questions.title: How much Microsoft telemetry should be blocked?
questions.desc: Windows sends diagnostic data to Microsoft continuously. The scope of what's acceptable differs by use case. Minimal blocks the DiagTrack service and the worst tracking. Standard adds advertising and activity feeds. Full Block adds every policy-controllable telemetry channel — including disabling DiagTrack, CEIP, TIPC, and telemetry scheduled tasks.
questions.note: Minimal: services.disable-diagtrack. Standard adds privacy.disable-advertising-id, privacy.disable-activity-feed, privacy.disable-tailored-experiences. Full Block adds all remaining privacy.* and tasks.disable-telemetry-tasks actions.

options.title: Minimal — stop DiagTrack service only
options.desc: Disables the Connected User Experiences and Telemetry service (DiagTrack). Covers the highest-volume telemetry channel.

options.title: Standard — block advertising and activity tracking
options.desc: Adds advertising ID opt-out, activity feed, tailored experiences, and typing insights on top of the minimal block.

options.title: Full Block — disable every telemetry channel
options.desc: Blocks all of the above plus telemetry scheduled tasks, MSRT opt-out, online speech, device monitoring, and app launch tracking.

questions.label: Activity Feed / Timeline
questions.title: Disable Windows Activity Feed (Timeline)?
questions.desc: The Activity Feed logs your app usage and document history to enable the Timeline feature. This data is also synced to Microsoft's servers if your account allows it. The feature was removed from the UI in Windows 11 but the logging layer still runs.
questions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System — EnableActivityFeed = 0, PublishUserActivities = 0.

options.title: Yes — disable Activity Feed
options.desc: Stops Windows from logging app and document history. Timeline (if present) will stop accumulating entries.

options.title: No — keep Activity Feed
options.desc: Windows logs your app activity. Required if you use Timeline or cross-device history features.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the Activity Feed enabled.

questions.label: Clipboard History
questions.title: Disable Clipboard History and cloud sync?
questions.desc: Clipboard History (Win+V) stores everything you copy. If cloud sync is enabled, this includes passwords and sensitive text sent to Microsoft's servers. community benchmarks and performance research both recommend disabling it unless you actively use the feature.
questions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System — AllowClipboardHistory = 0, AllowCrossDeviceClipboard = 0.

options.title: Yes — disable Clipboard History
options.desc: Clipboard works normally (copy/paste), but no history is stored locally or synced to the cloud.

options.title: No — keep Clipboard History
options.desc: Win+V lets you paste from previous clipboard entries. Useful for power users.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Clipboard History enabled.

questions.label: Location Services
questions.title: Disable Location Services?
questions.desc: Windows Location Services lets apps query your physical location. Unless you use maps or location-aware apps on this PC, this is unnecessary access to a privacy-sensitive sensor. community benchmarks marks this as an essential privacy tweak.
questions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LocationAndSensors — DisableLocation = 1.

options.title: Yes — disable Location Services
options.desc: No app (including Windows itself) can determine your physical location.

options.title: No — keep Location Services
options.desc: Apps can request your location. Required for maps, weather apps, and find-my-device.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Location Services enabled.

questions.label: Advertising ID
questions.title: Opt out of the Windows Advertising ID?
questions.desc: Windows assigns each user a unique advertising ID that apps use to track behaviour across sessions and deliver targeted ads. Opting out resets the ID and blocks new assignments. Both repos recommend this as a baseline privacy setting.
questions.note: HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\AdvertisingInfo — Enabled = 0.

options.title: Yes — opt out of Advertising ID
options.desc: Apps can no longer read your advertising ID. Store apps and ad-funded software lose cross-app tracking capability.

options.title: No — keep Advertising ID
options.desc: Apps can use the ID to deliver targeted ads and track cross-app behaviour.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the Advertising ID.

questions.label: Tailored Experiences
questions.title: Disable Tailored Experiences and diagnostic data usage?
questions.desc: Tailored Experiences uses your diagnostic data to suggest Microsoft features, tips, and offers. It is separate from general telemetry — this specifically controls whether diagnostic data feeds into product recommendations sent back to your device.
questions.note: HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Privacy — TailoredExperiencesWithDiagnosticDataEnabled = 0.

options.title: Yes — disable Tailored Experiences
options.desc: Microsoft stops using your diagnostic data to push product tips or feature suggestions.

options.title: No — allow tailored suggestions
options.desc: Microsoft may send personalised tips based on your diagnostic data.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to allow Tailored Experiences.

questions.label: Online Speech Recognition
questions.title: Disable Online Speech Recognition and personalisation?
questions.desc: Online Speech Recognition sends your voice to Microsoft's cloud for recognition. Speech personalisation builds a model of your voice and vocabulary. Both are disabled by default in enterprise but left enabled for consumer accounts.
questions.note: HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Speech_OneCore\Settings\OnlineSpeechPrivacy — HasAccepted = 0. Also sets privacy.disable-online-speech.

options.title: Yes — disable online speech features
options.desc: Voice commands still work locally, but nothing is sent to Microsoft's cloud for personalisation.

options.title: No — keep online speech features
options.desc: Microsoft builds a personalised speech model using your voice data.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep online speech personalisation.

questions.label: Delivery Optimization
questions.title: Disable Delivery Optimization (P2P update sharing)?
questions.desc: Delivery Optimization can upload Windows Update data to other PCs on the internet, using your bandwidth. While it also helps you download updates faster from peers, on a metered or gaming connection the upload overhead is undesirable. Both repos recommend disabling it.
questions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\DeliveryOptimization — DODownloadMode = 0.

options.title: Yes — disable Delivery Optimization
options.desc: Updates download from Microsoft servers only. No P2P upload of your cached update data.

options.title: No — keep P2P update sharing
options.desc: Windows may use your connection to seed updates to other PCs. Can speed up your own downloads on some networks.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Delivery Optimization enabled.

questions.label: Error Reporting
questions.title: Disable Windows Error Reporting (WER)?
questions.desc: When an app crashes, WER collects crash dumps and sends them to Microsoft. This is useful for developers but reveals crash information and app data to Microsoft on consumer machines. Disabling it also stops the WerSvc service from running.
questions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\Windows Error Reporting — Disabled = 1. Also disables services.disable-wer-service.

options.title: Yes — disable error reporting
options.desc: Crash data stays local. No dumps are sent to Microsoft. Apps crash silently without triggering the WER dialog.

options.title: No — keep error reporting
options.desc: Crash dumps are sent to Microsoft. Useful if you're debugging software or want app vendors to get your crash reports.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows Error Reporting enabled.

questions.label: Consumer Cloud Content
questions.title: Disable Consumer Features and cloud-pushed content?
questions.desc: Windows Consumer Features installs suggested apps, adds promotional tiles to the Start menu, and enables cloud-delivered content like lock screen tips and spotlight ads. community benchmarks's DisableWindowsConsumerFeatures flag removes this layer entirely.
questions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CloudContent — DisableWindowsConsumerFeatures = 1. Also disables privacy.disable-cloud-content and tasks.disable-cloud-content-tasks.

options.title: Yes — disable cloud-pushed content
options.desc: No more suggested apps appearing in Start, no promotional tiles, no lock screen 'fun facts'.

options.title: No — allow cloud content
options.desc: Microsoft may push app suggestions and promotional content to Start and the lock screen.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to allow cloud-pushed consumer content.

questions.label: Find My Device
questions.title: Disable Find My Device?
questions.desc: Find My Device requires location tracking to be active and transmits the PC's location to Microsoft. Unless you genuinely need to locate a lost laptop, this is unnecessary surveillance. community benchmarks includes this in its essential privacy block.
questions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\FindMyDevice — AllowFindMyDevice = 0. Also applies privacy.disable-find-my-device.

options.title: Yes — disable Find My Device
options.desc: Location is not transmitted for device tracking. You won't be able to locate this PC via Microsoft account.

options.title: No — keep Find My Device
options.desc: Useful on laptops. Requires Location Services to be enabled.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Find My Device enabled.

questions.label: PowerShell Telemetry
questions.title: Opt out of PowerShell telemetry?
questions.desc: PowerShell 7+ sends command usage data to Microsoft by default unless the POWERSHELL_TELEMETRY_OPTOUT environment variable is set. community benchmarks includes this as an essential tweak.
questions.note: Sets the POWERSHELL_TELEMETRY_OPTOUT=1 system environment variable. Applies to PS7+ only — Windows PowerShell 5.1 is unaffected.

options.title: Yes — opt out of PS telemetry
options.desc: PowerShell 7+ stops sending command analytics. The opt-out persists via a system environment variable.

options.title: No — keep PS telemetry
options.desc: PowerShell 7+ continues to report command usage to Microsoft.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep PowerShell telemetry.

chapters.title: Performance
chapters.description: CPU, GPU, memory, and timer optimizations.

questions.label: Fullscreen Optimizations
questions.title: Disable Fullscreen Optimizations (FSO)?
questions.desc: Fullscreen Optimizations intercept exclusive fullscreen games and silently run them in a borderless window. This enables faster Alt+Tab and overlay injection but adds a compositor layer between the game and the display driver, increasing input latency. performance research recommends disabling FSO for exclusive fullscreen apps.
questions.note: HKCU\System\GameConfigStore — GameDVR_FSEBehaviorMode = 2, GameDVR_DXGIHonorFSEWindowsCompatible = 1.

options.title: Yes — disable FSO
options.desc: Games run in true exclusive fullscreen mode. Lower input latency, but Alt+Tab is slower and some overlays may not work.

options.title: No — keep Fullscreen Optimizations
options.desc: Windows wraps exclusive fullscreen in a borderless window. Faster Alt+Tab, easier overlays.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Fullscreen Optimizations enabled.

questions.label: Core Parking
questions.title: Disable CPU core parking?
questions.desc: Core parking powers down CPU cores that aren't in heavy use to save energy. When a burst of work arrives, parked cores must wake up — adding latency spikes. performance research disables core parking via power plan configuration to keep all cores ready at all times.
questions.note: Sets HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power\PowerSettings — MinValueMax/MaxValueMax for core parking to 0 (0 parked cores).

options.title: Yes — disable core parking
options.desc: All CPU cores stay awake. Eliminates wake-up latency when workload bursts. Slightly higher idle power draw.

options.title: No — allow core parking
options.desc: Idle cores are powered down. Saves energy. May add latency on workload spikes.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to allow core parking.

chapters.title: Shell & UI
chapters.description: Explorer, taskbar, Start menu, and visual tweaks.

questions.label: Copilot
questions.title: Disable and remove Copilot?
questions.desc: Copilot adds a persistent sidebar button to the taskbar and runs background services even when not in use. Both repos recommend removing it. This covers the Copilot taskbar button, the Copilot app package, and its autostart entries.
questions.note: HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced — ShowCopilotButton = 0. Also removes appx.remove-copilot and appx.remove-copilot-app.

options.title: Yes — disable and remove Copilot
options.desc: Copilot button disappears from the taskbar and the app package is removed.

options.title: No — keep Copilot
options.desc: Copilot stays visible and accessible via the taskbar button.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Copilot.

questions.label: Visual Transparency
questions.title: Disable transparency effects in the UI?
questions.desc: Transparency (acrylic, mica) requires the GPU to composite blurred samples of whatever is behind each window. It is a continuous GPU tax that exists purely for aesthetics. Both repos recommend disabling it to reclaim GPU headroom.
questions.note: HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Themes\Personalize — EnableTransparency = 0.

options.title: Yes — disable transparency
options.desc: Taskbar, Start, and window chrome become opaque. Slightly lower GPU load in the desktop compositor.

options.title: No — keep transparency effects
options.desc: Acrylic and mica effects remain on taskbar and windows.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep transparency effects.

questions.label: Nagle Algorithm
questions.title: Disable Nagle's algorithm for lower network latency?
questions.desc: Nagle's algorithm buffers small TCP packets and combines them before sending. This reduces bandwidth overhead but adds latency — every small packet waits up to 200ms to be bundled. performance research disables it for gaming to ensure packets are sent immediately.
questions.note: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip\Parameters\Interfaces\{adapter} — TcpAckFrequency = 1, TCPNoDelay = 1.

options.title: Yes — disable Nagle (lower latency)
options.desc: Small TCP packets are sent immediately. Reduces ping jitter in online games at the cost of slightly more packets.

options.title: No — keep Nagle enabled
options.desc: TCP coalesces small packets. Better for bulk transfers, adds small latency to interactive traffic.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Nagle's algorithm enabled.

questions.label: Windows Defender
questions.title: What should happen with Windows Defender?
questions.desc: Windows Defender provides real-time antivirus protection. It consumes CPU/RAM and can interfere with game file access. You can keep it, reduce its footprint by disabling cloud-based lookups, or disable it entirely — which performance research calls a significant security risk and marks as default FALSE.
questions.note: Reduce: security.disable-defender-cloud (DisableBehaviorMonitoring, SpynetReporting=0). Full disable: security.full-defender-disable, security.disable-defender-realtime. Requires VBS/Tamper Protection to be off first.

options.title: Keep Defender — full protection
options.desc: Real-time protection, cloud lookups, and automatic sample submission all remain active.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Defender fully active.

options.title: Reduce — disable cloud lookups and MAPS
options.desc: Local real-time scanning stays on. Cloud-based lookups and automatic sample submission are disabled — reducing telemetry without losing local scanning.

warnings: Cloud-based threat intelligence is disabled. Zero-day malware detected only by cloud lookups may not be caught.

options.title: Full Disable — turn off all real-time protection
options.desc: Defender is completely disabled. No real-time scanning of any kind.

warnings: You will have no antivirus protection. Malware, ransomware, and trojans will run undetected.
This requires Tamper Protection and VBS to already be disabled.
performance research labels this default: FALSE. Only do this with a third-party AV in place.

questions.label: SmartScreen
questions.title: Disable Microsoft SmartScreen?
questions.desc: SmartScreen checks every downloaded file and URL against Microsoft's reputation database. This provides real malware protection but also sends file hashes and URLs to Microsoft. Disabling it removes the protection and the telemetry. Only do this if you rely on other security tools.
questions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System — EnableSmartScreen = 0. Also applies privacy.disable-smartscreen.

options.title: Yes — disable SmartScreen
options.desc: File reputation checks stop. No file hash or URL is sent to Microsoft for screening. You lose a real layer of malware protection.

warnings: SmartScreen blocks real malware. Disabling it removes a meaningful protection layer. Only do this if you have alternative endpoint protection.

options.title: No — keep SmartScreen
options.desc: Downloaded files and URLs are checked against Microsoft's cloud reputation database.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep SmartScreen enabled.

questions.label: Windows Update
questions.title: Fully block Windows Update?
questions.desc: performance research documents blocking Windows Update as a way to prevent unexpected reboots and background bandwidth usage. It explicitly marks this as a SECURITY RISK with default FALSE. Blocking updates leaves your system without security patches indefinitely.
questions.note: Applies security.disable-update-asap and system.defer-feature-updates. Full block additionally sets the WU service to disabled and blocks the WU firewall rules.

options.title: Allow updates — keep Windows Update
options.desc: Security patches and feature updates install normally. Recommended for all users.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows Update fully active.

options.title: Defer — pause feature updates, allow security patches
options.desc: Feature updates are deferred 365 days. Security patches still install. Auto-restart is suppressed during active hours.

warnings: Feature updates will not install for up to 365 days. Security patches still apply.

options.title: Block — disable all Windows Update
options.desc: Windows Update service is disabled. No patches of any kind will install until you manually re-enable it.

warnings: You will receive no security patches. Known vulnerabilities will remain unpatched indefinitely.
performance research explicitly marks this default: FALSE and labels it a SECURITY RISK.
Only appropriate for air-gapped or isolated gaming machines.
